---
title: "Yee Seng Chan"
pagetitle: "Yee Seng Chan | Applied AI"
description-meta: "Applied AI scientist working on information extraction, evaluation, and the reliability of LLM systems."
page-layout: full
format:
  html:
    body-classes: home
about:
  template: trestles
  image: ys_profile.png
  image-alt: "Portrait of Yee Seng Chan"
  image-shape: round
  image-width: 13rem
  links:
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/chanys
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://linkedin.com/in/yeesengchan
    - icon: rss
      text: Writing feed
      href: writing.xml
---

I work on reliable LLM systems: systems whose behavior can be measured, inspected, and improved.

My background is a decade of NLP and information extraction, much of it deep-learning-based, judged by external evaluation, including work as a principal investigator on DARPA and IARPA programs at Raytheon BBN. The years since have been at AI startups, leading NLP and LLM work in production, most recently on LLM workflows for clinical documentation and structured extraction.

The subject is now LLMs, but the engineering question is familiar: how do you build a system whose outputs can be trusted?

## Start here

For my current positioning on LLM reliability and evaluation, read [The agent harness](posts/series/the-agent-harness/index.qmd) and [LLM evaluation, honestly](posts/series/llm-evaluation-honestly/index.qmd). These series explain how I think about the system around the model: state, tools, gates, traces, labels, judges, regression cases, and eval drift.

For the throughline from my earlier NLP work to current LLM workflows, read [Information extraction didn't disappear. It moved inside the workflow](posts/series/research-foundations-of-modern-llms/05-information-extraction/index.qmd).

The rest is grouped by argument under [Series](series.qmd). There is also a [list of projects](projects.qmd) and [more about me](about.qmd).
